channel.title: NYT > World News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Here’s How Iran Could Respond to Renewed U.S.-Israeli Strikes
item.description: In any new round of fighting, Iranian officials could adopt new tactics, including intensified strikes on neighbors and trying to close off a second strait.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Iranians have used their proximity to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical trade waterway, to exert immense pressure on the global economy.

item.title: Trump’s Portrait Pops Up on Rickshaws in India to Mark America’s 250th
item.description: The president is popping up on the back of the iconic three-wheeler to kick off America’s 250th anniversary celebrations in India.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.media:credit: Anindito Mukherjee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Md. Tayemkhan, a rickshaw driver, waited for hours near the U.S. Embassy in New Delhi to get a banner featuring President Donald Trump put on the back of his vehicle. He said he was paid about $16.

item.title: Oil Slick Reaches a Pristine Persian Gulf Island in Iran
item.description: Videos show birds, turtles and crabs trapped inside mounds of tar around Shidvar island, a protected wildlife sanctuary with turquoise waters and white sand beaches.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Erika Solomon and Devon Lum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A video verified by The Times shows oil coating the shoreline of Shidvar Island as smoke rises from the Lavan refinery, seen in the distance.

item.title: In Closed-Door Talks, U.S. Demands a Major Role in Greenland
item.description: Greenlandic officials worry about the direction of the negotiations aimed at defusing President Trump’s threats to seize their island. But they have little leverage.
item.dc:creator: Jeffrey Gettleman, Maya Tekeli, Anton Troianovski and Eric Schmitt
item.media:credit: Ivor Prickett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ilulisaat, Greenland, a town where a Chinese state company nearly won a contract to build an airport in 2018. After U.S. officials pressured Denmark to step in, Greenland opted for a Danish company.

item.title: Blocked Strait of Hormuz Leads to New Opportunities for Syria
item.description: The latest war in the Middle East has created new economic opportunities for Syria thanks to its geography.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Iraqi trucks carrying oil along the highway near the Baniyas port and refinery in Syria in April.

item.title: Trump’s Taiwan Gambit is Already a Gift to China
item.description: President Trump’s open willingness to hold up a $14 billion Taiwan arms package is a win for Beijing. Now China could be weighing how to keep the weapons on ice for as long as it can.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump with the Chinese leader, Xi Jinping, in Beijing, on Thursday.

item.title: He Shut Liquor Stores and Banned Abortion, All for the Glory of Russia
item.description: A firebrand governor aims to transform his region into a laboratory for the Kremlin’s reactionary ideals.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko and Mary Gelman
item.media:description: A mural reading “the powerhouse of the Russian world” in the city of Vologda, Russia, in February.

item.title: Trump Warns Iran the ‘Clock Is Ticking’ as Tensions Surge Again in Middle East
item.description: The president has sought to force Iran to accept his terms on its nuclear program or else face renewed war. An emboldened Iran has rebuffed Trump’s demands.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump on Friday. He said in a social media post on Sunday that Iran had to move fast “or there won’t be anything left,” adding “the Clock is Ticking.”

item.title: Ebola Was Identified in Congo Weeks Before W.H.O. Declared an Emergency
item.description: Early surveillance and testing failed to identify the rare species of Ebola responsible for the current outbreak. An American doctor is among the confirmed cases.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: At a hospital in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of a new Ebola outbreak.

item.title: Millions Recovered in FIFA Corruption Scandal May Now Be Missing Again Before World Cup
item.description: Ahead of the World Cup, the head of South American soccer faces an ethics complaint he received payments recovered from a 2015 investigation that shook global soccer.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: Confederation of North, Central American and Caribbean Association Football (CONCACAF)
item.media:description: Alejandro Dominguez, the president of Conmebol, the South American soccer federation, in Quito, Ecuador, last year.

item.title: Soccer Star Kylian Mbappé Faces Off Against France’s Far Right
item.description: Kylian Mbappé, the captain of France’s national team, set off a storm a month before the World Cup by suggesting that the National Rally party was a threat to France.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:description: “I know what it means, and what kind of consequences it can have for my country when those kinds of people take control,” Kylian Mbappé said of the French far-right in a magazine interview.

item.title: Russia’s New Human Rights Commissioner Accused of Helping Kidnap Ukrainian Children
item.description: Yana Lantratova was instrumental in helping the chairman of her party to illegally adopt an infant girl from Russian-occupied Ukraine, the Ukrainian authorities say.
item.dc:creator: Nataliya Vasilyeva
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Yana Lantratova, in Moscow in October, will report directly to President Vladimir V. Putin of Russia.

item.title: 2 Americans Detained in Japan After Entering Punch the Monkey’s Zoo Enclosure
item.description: The men were charged with obstructing operations at a zoo, apparently as a stunt connected to a cryptocurrency. They denied the charges.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Hisako Ueno
item.media:credit: Kentaro Takahashi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Visitors at the Ichikawa City Zoo in Japan last month. The site is the home of Punch, a 9-month-old macaque who has become an internet sensation.

item.title: U.S. Airstrikes Kill ISIS Fighters in Nigeria, Officials Say
item.description: U.S. military launched attack in coordination with Nigerian forces days after President Trump said a joint operation had killed Islamic State’s global leader.
item.dc:creator: Saikou Jammeh and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Islamic State in Iraq and Syria (ISIS)

item.title: Nauru Hopes You Will Call It by a New Name
item.description: Tiny Nauru could soon be called Naoero.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather

item.title: U.S. Hits Top Cuban Officials With Sanctions
item.description: The Trump administration, ratcheting up its effort to cripple the Cuban government, also imposed sanctions on three government agencies, including the police.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:description: The Cuban capital this week.

item.title: Étienne Davignon Dies Before Trial in Patrice Lumumba Killing
item.description: Étienne Davignon had been set to stand trial in connection with the murder of Patrice Lumumba, the first prime minister of what is now the Democratic Republic of Congo.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.media:credit: Benoit Doppagne/BELGA, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Étienne Davignon in 2018.

item.title: South Korea and Japan’s Leaders Are Set to Meet: What to Know
item.description: Japan’s prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, and South Korea’s president, Lee Jae Myung, have surprised many observers by moving past their nations’ historical grievances.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: Japan’s prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, arriving at the Daegu International Airport in South Korea on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Threatens Iran and Then Pulls Back, All in the Same Day
item.description: President Trump has repeatedly said he’ll restart military action against Iran, only to stop short of plunging the United States directly back into an unpopular war.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A U.S. military official said that Iran had been able to study the flight patterns of American fighter jets and bombers.

item.title: When Nationalism Beats Nationalism
item.description: A progressive, pluralistic nationalism has prevailed at the polls in Scotland and Wales. Could it gain traction elsewhere?
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold

item.title: Scientists Tweaked the Global Warming Outlook. So Trump Weighed In.
item.description: Renewable energy has helped make the worst-case scenario a bit less bad. The president said, falsely, it shows that climate scientists were wrong all along.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:description: Solar panels and wind turbines in north-central China. Renewable is becoming more affordable in many countries.

item.title: Shakira Is Found Not Guilty of Tax Fraud in Spain
item.description: A Spanish court ruled that the pop star was not a tax resident of Spain in 2011 and ordered the country’s tax authorities to return tens of millions of dollars to her.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses and Carlos Barragán
item.media:description: The pop star Shakira has been embroiled in a yearslong tax battle with the Spanish authorities.

item.title: Bodies of Four Italian Divers Found in Maldives Cave
item.description: The discovery comes after days of searching by an international team of rescue divers. A fifth body was recovered last week.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins and Maahil Mohamed
item.media:credit: Maldives President's Media Division, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A coast guard boat and other vessels deployed to search for missing Italian divers near Alimathaa Island in the Maldives, on Saturday.

item.title: Louvre Announces Architects for Expansion That Will Include New Mona Lisa Space
item.description: A team of international architects has been selected for the project, which is expected to increase the Paris museum’s capacity by three million visitors a year.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Antoine Boureau/Hans Lucas, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The Louvre’s current gallery displaying the Mona Lisa is often packed with visitors.

item.title: G7 Finance Ministers Look to Contain Iran Economic Fallout
item.description: Top policymakers were expected to discuss rising energy prices and sanctions policy at a critical summit in Paris this week.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: China’s leader, Xi Jinping, greeting Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent last week in Beijing. Mr. Bessent is leading the U.S. delegation at the G7 finance ministers’ meeting in Paris this week.

item.title: Actually, Democracy Dies in H.R.
item.description: New research sheds light on how mediocre employees help would-be authoritarians maintain power.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Taub
item.category: Making a Career in Dictatorship: The Secret Logic behind Repression and Coups (Book)
item.media:description: Argentine security forces seizing a young man in 1982 in Buenos Aires during the Dirty War.

item.title: Catastrophe Is Emerging in the World’s Most Vulnerable Places
item.description: The humanitarian relief system, decimated by cuts, faces a grave challenge as the Middle East war causes soaring costs for food, fuel and fertilizer.
item.dc:creator: Peter S. Goodman and Finbarr O’Reilly
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Muslima Ibrahim Mohamed, 38, holding her 2-day-old son, Noor Mohamed.

item.title: What to Know About the Ebola Outbreak, as U.S. Restricts Travelers
item.description: The United States restricted entry of people who have been to three African countries, after the World Health Organization declared a global health emergency. The outbreak has killed more than 100.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:description: A motorcycle taxi driver at a hospital in Goma, Democratic Republic of Congo, on Sunday.

item.title: He’s Forging the Future of War
item.description: Meet the man who wants Ukraine to use autonomous drones that could decide on their own to use lethal force.
item.dc:creator: Katrin Bennhold
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Brendan Hoffman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mykhailo Fedorov, Ukraine’s defense minister. is pushing the military to adopt technology more quickly.

item.title: Runoff in Peru Offers Two Starkly Differing Visions for the Nation
item.description: The presidential election pits Keiko Fujimori, a conservative, against Roberto Sánchez, a leftist, after a crisis marked by delays and protests.
item.dc:creator: Mitra Taj and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Sara Wayra for The New York Times
item.media:description: Keiko Fujimori, the leader of the Popular Force Party, at a campaign rally last month in Lima, Peru.

item.title: Wave of Ukrainian Strikes Kills at Least 4, Russia Says
item.description: More than 550 drones were intercepted or shot down in more than a dozen regions, including Moscow, in one of largest attacks of the war.
item.dc:creator: Valerie Hopkins and Cassandra Vinograd
item.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
item.media:description: A damaged residential building in Zelenograd, in Russia’s Moscow region, after a drone strike on Sunday.

item.title: How Birding Apps Are Boosting Tourism in the World’s Top Bird Destination
item.description: Colombia is home to the most species of birds known to ornithologists. Merlin, an app, is helping to build an “avian tourism” industry there.
item.dc:creator: Max Bearak and Esteban Vanegas
item.media:description: Samantha Giraldo and Bibiana Acevedo looking for birds in Los Guácharos nature preserve in Jardín, Colombia.

item.title: In Iraqi Desert, Two Israeli Outposts Were Kept Secret for Months
item.description: Israel spent over a year preparing a covert site in Iraq for its operations against Iran, regional officials say. Iraqi officials later confirmed the existence of a second base.
item.dc:creator: Erika Solomon and Falih Hassan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A shepherd with his herd in the Najaf desert in southwestern Iraq on Tuesday. Israel operated at least two secret outposts in the desert during the war with Iran.

item.title: The Iran War Is Crippling One of the World’s Wealthiest Nations
item.description: Iranian attacks and the stoppage of seaborne transit have paralyzed Qatar’s vital gas exports, stalling the economic pivots intended to anchor the country’s growth.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Qatar has tried to transform itself into a tourist destination and a hub for international business and finance.

item.title: Erdogan Wants Turkey to Have More Babies. Few Parents Are Listening.
item.description: Using cash grants and subsidized loans, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan’s government is trying to increase the birthrate. It’s not working.
item.dc:creator: Ben Hubbard and Safak Timur
item.media:credit: Emin Ozmen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A family visiting Miniaturk, a theme park in Istanbul. Living costs have undermined efforts to increase fertility rates.

item.title: A North Korean Soccer Team Makes a Rare Visit to South Korea
item.description: The team’s trip, for a women’s soccer tournament, is unlikely to catalyze a diplomatic thaw between the two Koreas, although some sports events have.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of Naegohyang, a North Korean women’s soccer team, arriving at Incheon International Airport in South Korea on Sunday.

item.title: W.H.O. Declares Ebola Outbreak a Global Health Emergency
item.description: The agency made the declaration just a day after Africa’s top public health agency first announced the outbreak. There is no approved vaccine for this species of Ebola.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang, Matthew Mpoke Bigg and Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: A man is carried from an ambulance on Saturday as he arrives at a hospital after confirmation of an Ebola outbreak in the Ituri province of the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: Venezuela Extradites Billionaire Tycoon Alex Saab to U.S.
item.description: The extradition of Alex Saab, who is tied to a huge graft scheme, is part of a purge of powerful figures who helped the deposed president, Nicolás Maduro, stay in power.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:description: Alex Saab, center, in Caracas, Venezuela, in 2023, was once among key allies of the deposed Venezuelan president, Nicolás Maduro.

item.title: Félicien Kabuga Dies; Accused of Masterminding Rwanda’s Genocide
item.description: One of his country’s richest businessmen, he fled arrest and escaped prosecution for allegedly financing and directing the bloodletting that cost 800,000 lives in 1994.
item.dc:creator: Alan Cowell
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:description: A screen grab of Félicien Kabuga at a hearing in The Hague, where he faced charges of genocide and crimes against humanity, in 2022.

item.title: Car Plows Into Pedestrians in Northern Italian Town, Injuring at Least 8
item.description: Four of the injured were in serious condition, the mayor of Modena said, praising residents who rushed to stop the car’s driver from escaping on foot.
item.dc:creator: Elisabetta Povoledo
item.media:description: The police patrolling the scene after a car crashed into pedestrians in Modena, Italy, on Saturday.

item.title: Trump Calls Xi a ‘Friend.’ But He Left China Without Any Breakthroughs.
item.description: The lack of concrete agreements with Beijing shows the risks of President Trump’s personality-driven foreign policy, which rests on the belief that he can defend U.S. interests through charm and force of will.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.media:description: President Trump and China’s leader, Xi Jinping, at the state banquet in Beijing on Thursday.

item.title: Fears Grow That Iran May Be Using Proxy Groups Beyond Mideast
item.description: The charges against Mohammad al-Saadi in the United States have raised concerns that Iran could be working with its proxies to stage attacks outside the region.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman, Neil MacFarquhar and Falih Hassan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The funeral of a commander from the Kataib Hezbollah paramilitary group in Baghdad in 2024. The group is backed by Iran, and its fighters have killed American soldiers and kidnapped people in the past.

item.title: Train Crashes Into Bangkok Traffic, Killing at Least 8
item.description: A freight train plowed into traffic on train tracks, starting a fire. The authorities did not immediately give a cause but said a barrier had not come down.
item.dc:creator: Kittiphum Sringammuang, Isabella Kwai and Phuriphat Dejsuphong
item.media:description: Emergency services at the site of the collision in Bangkok on Saturday.

item.title: Man Stole Medieval Skull From Church and Sealed it in Concrete, Police Say
item.description: The man disagreed with the 800-year-old relic’s display in a church in the Czech Republic and planned to throw it into a river, police said.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Police of The Czech Republic, via Reuters
item.media:description: A relic, said to be the skull of Saint Zdislava of Lemberk, in an image released on Wednesday by the police in the Czech Republic.

item.title: China Will Host Putin, Days After Trump’s Visit
item.description: The Russian leader will go to Beijing on Tuesday for talks with President Xi Jinping. The Kremlin said he had watched President Trump’s visit closely.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Nechepurenko
item.media:description: President Vladimir V. Putin’s China visit will be “a good opportunity to share opinions on the contacts that the Chinese had with the Americans,” the Kremlin’s spokesman said.

item.title: Finishing School
item.description: It’s graduation season, a time of aspiration and anxiety. Today is about the aspiration — the wisdom to be gleaned from great commencement addresses.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: How a Times Reporter Found Cartel Gold at the Royal Canadian Mint
item.description: A conversation with Justin Scheck, who found that illegal Colombian gold transformed into legal North American gold through bureaucratic sleights of hand, and ended up in Canada.
item.dc:creator: Shawna Richer
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: This illegal mine near Caucasia, Colombia, sells gold into the Royal Canadian Mint’s supply chain.

item.title: They Fled to Safety in Palestinian Territory, Then Settlers Attacked Again
item.description: Violent settlers are not merely clearing Palestinians from land under Israel’s control. They are attacking areas where Israel agreed to Palestinian self-governance.
item.dc:creator: Azam Ahmed and Ivor Prickett
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Ivor Prickett for The New York Times

item.title: Rubio, Once a China Hawk, Strikes Softer Tone to Align With Trump
item.description: As a senator, Marco Rubio even hinted at the need for regime change in China. Now he talks about cooperation.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong and Michael Crowley
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:description: Marco Rubio, President Trump’s secretary of state and national security adviser, posed a sticky problem ahead of Mr. Trump’s first visit to China in his second term.

item.title: Rival Protests Take Place in London, With a Major Security Effort
item.description: The police have deployed thousands of officers, partly to keep far-right and pro-Palestinian marchers apart. The events drew tens of thousands to London.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear and Megan Specia
item.media:description: Supporters of the British far-right activist Tommy Robinson at the “Unite the Kingdom” march in London on Saturday.

item.title: Which Countries Are Profiting From the Iran War Oil Shock
item.description: An analysis of oil export data offers clues about which nations have benefited from higher prices, and which have lost a lot of revenue.
item.dc:creator: Amy Fan and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: How a Hantavirus Outbreak Turned a Nature Cruise Into a Nightmare
item.description: The hantavirus outbreak on the MV Hondius set off alarms for a world still traumatized by Covid. For those on board, the danger was much closer.
item.dc:creator: Jason Horowitz, Claire Moses, Amelia Nierenberg and Samuel Granados

item.title: Trump Says a Top ISIS Leader Was Killed in a U.S.-Nigerian Mission
item.description: Abu Bilal al-Minuki, said to be the Islamic State’s second-ranking leader, was killed after a helicopter-borne assault by commandos from both countries on Friday night, two U.S. officials said.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Regalado and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: President Trump announced the operation in a late-night post on Truth Social, his social media platform.

item.title: Trump Uses Taiwan Arms Sales as Bargaining Chip With China, in a Risky Move
item.description: The president said a potential arms deal for Taiwan was a “very good negotiating chip” in talks with Beijing. His words raise questions about the reliability of U.S. support.
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley
item.media:description: Patriot missile systems on display during a Taiwanese military exercise in Taipei last year.

item.title: It’s Been a Wild Week in British Politics. What Happens Next?
item.description: No one has challenged Prime Minister Keir Starmer for the leadership — yet. But one rival has a path to Downing Street, and another says he’ll join the fray if there is one.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Members of the news media congregated outside 10 Downing Street on Thursday, after days of turmoil in the Labour Party over Prime Minister Keir Starmer’s leadership.

item.title: The Push to Indict Raúl Castro, Cuba’s Former President
item.description: With Raúl Castro, Cuba’s former president, possibly facing charges in the 1996 killings, here’s a look back at what happened that day.
item.dc:creator: Frances Robles
item.media:description: Former President Raúl Castro of Cuba attending a May Day parade in Havana this month.

item.title: With Possible Raúl Castro Indictment, U.S. Eyes Venezuela Playbook
item.description: Amid stepped-up surveillance flights, a visit of the C.I.A. director and an energy embargo, the White House is trying to increase pressure on Cuba.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes, Tyler Pager, Eric Schmitt, Michael Crowley and Frances Robles
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Raúl Castro is the former president of Cuba and younger brother of Fidel Castro.
